channel_name: The Prof G Show · Scott Galloway
video_title: Hong Kong's AI Crackdown, Lululemon’s Marketing Backlash, and World Cup Fever | China Decode
transcript: I've never lived in a country that is as football mad as China. And I come from the UK, but China is more football mad, I would like to say, than the UK. People are fascinated by it. So, for China not to have qualified for the World Cup uh since 2002, obviously, it's a big disappointment for Chinese people. >> Welcome to China Decode. I'm Alice Han. >> And I'm James King. >> In today's episode of China Decode, we're discussing private companies limiting the use of AI models in Hong Kong, a Lululemon campaign that goes wrong, and World Cup fever takes over despite the Chinese team not qualifying this year. That's all coming up. But first, let's do a quick check-in with how the markets are starting the week in China. On Monday, markets were once again open following the Dragon Boat Festival holiday and saw a strong rebound. The Shanghai Composite closed up 1.78% and the Shenzhen Component finished the day up 2.13%. Its highest level in 11 years. The People's Bank of China announced it would keep its key lending rates steady. Notable gains included Bank of China up 2.8%, 8% narrow technology a semiconductor equipment company up 3.3% and electric battery maker CL up 4.4%. All right, let's get into it. JP Morgan Chase has reportedly cut off its employees in Hong Kong from accessing anthropics AI models, citing concerns over licensing terms that restrict use in greater China. Goldman Sachs has already made a similar move in the region. At the same time, Washington is escalating pressure on Dutch chip maker ASML over concerns that advanced chipm lithography equipment could have reached China. A reminder that the battle over AI isn't just about software, but also the hardware needed to power it. So, James, I thought it was super interesting to have those two stories really come to the four in the pretty much the same week. And it reminds me that, you know, we are living in a technological cold war where there is an iron curtain increasingly developing uh being drawn up between mainly China and the US. you know, we can debate as to whether or not, you know, Anthropic has done this move to, you know, cut off access to China and now Hong Kong because it wants to be seen as anti-China and pro- US or if it's cowtowing and pressure from Washington. But what is clear to me is that increasingly uh Hong Kong is getting looped into mainland China. This I think may be net bad for the financial services sector in Hong Kong which relies on being a crossber hub not just of funds and financing as you know but also of talent and IP. Uh and then more than that the ASML story just reminds me the stakes are not just about uh the models themselves. They're also about the hardware used to to power them. Now, uh, ASML is saying that they they want it all involved. But the fact that the Americans are going hard after them, uh, suggests that in this kind of tech race, not just over models, but increasingly over chips, I feel as though the US is getting more and more worried both in the private sector and public sector in terms of maybe China catching up or China really having real cards to play that we've discussed previously. But James, do you agree with that? >> Yes, absolutely. I mean, of the two um two strands that you mentioned, Alice, I would say the ASML allegation uh by the US government is probably the more significant. We don't know if it's true at the moment. Uh but the fact that the US um uh Department of Commerce is escalating its confrontation uh today, that's uh Monday, in Europe, uh with ASML on this topic. seems to me to show that uh the US is at the very least very angry about this. These are both parts of the bigger tech war tech rivalry between the US and China. But of the two, I would say the ability of uh the west to make the world's most advanced semiconductors and China uh which is currently unable to make those really advanced semiconductors is the most uh important. It's the area where the gap between China and the US remains uh the biggest at the moment. And if it's true, and I stress if it's true that the world's most advanced chipm which are manufactured by ASML have actually been sent to uh China, then this is a massive gamecher because these ASML machines and I've actually seen them. I've been to the ASML factory in Einhovven and I've seen the most advanced machine they make. They are simply unique. No other company, no other country in the world can make these so-called EUV uh machines. And without them, you simply can't make the world's most advanced semiconductors. So, if it's true, it's a massive issue. ASML has denied it. They have rejected every element of the US allegation. So, it's a very very uh uh emphatic denial. They say they've never shipped an EUV machine to China and they've added that they've never shipped any component module or equipment specifically designed to be used in an EUV machine. So that's a a really strong denial. There's no wiggle room there as far as I can make out uh for weasel words. But as I mentioned, the US Department of Commerce has escalated its confrontation today. They are presenting specific documentary evidence uh that the company shipped specialized transport equipment and other components compatible with extreme ultraviolet lithography to Chinese entities. Now the fact that the US um uh department of commerce is escalating says quite a lot to me. This basically shows that the tech war between the US and China remains very much in force. In spite of the mllifying summit that we saw between Trump and Cining in recent weeks, this tech war remains virulent. Now these LLMs and particularly Anthropic uh the issue as you mentioned Alice is Hong Kong. the mainland uh already cannot get hold of anthropics, LLMs, the large language models, these uh extraordinary AI programs. Um but now JP Morgan Chase is restricting Hong Kong uh from uh using the different LLMs that Anthropic makes. Um, and so this means that not only is China uh putting restrictions on the US in what the US can can use when it comes to LLM, but also the US is we know uh for a long time has been putting restrictions on China. But now Hong Kong is part of the whole picture as well. Restrictions on the use of these LLMs in Hong Kong is now very much uh part of the picture. And this goes against what Hong Kong was specifically arranged to be in the past. Hong Kong has always been a special case. It's designated as a special economic zone with China. It's got a high degree of autonomy, um, etc., etc., but now the US is treating Hong Kong pretty much as just another part of China. I'm puzzled because to what extent is this decision by Anthropic and Open prior to Anthropic's announcement to basically cordon off Hong Kong and treat it like mainland a political decision or is it a competition concern? What I mean by that is, are they motivated to lump Hong Kong with mainland China because they see China as a big risk? Now, Anthropic has has been very public in saying that it is pro-American AI and believes that it it is it should be at the vanguard in the competition, national security and technology competition with China or is it really you know anthropic and open AI very worried about distillation because if it's this latter scenario then there are so many uh Chinese engineers tech companies that exist outside of mainland China you look to Singapore or are you going to ext extend to that? Are you going to extend to the you know many hundreds of thousands of Chinese engineers and top level talents in Silicon Valley say are you going to extend that even to your employees? Now Antropic just came out and we haven't mentioned it yet but I think this is quite significant. It came out and said well the Chinese nationals who are working for a company can't be working on fa fable and mythos. They're barred from working on that. you know, to what extent is that actually happening remains to be seen, but it it seems that they're taking that drastic action too to cut off some of the talent um having uh proximity to the cutting edge frontier models, fable and and and mythos. Um so I I'm I'm not really sure how this plays out, but generally let's take the other side on Hong Kong's perspective. I think this is not great. you know about I think somewhere in the line of a quarter of Hong Kong's GDP is in financial services and it employs 250,000 people this is a significant part of the Hong Kong story and if you basically ensure that the financial services and tech companies in Hong Kong don't have access to leading models but also are not uh interoperable or plugged in with the rest of the company outside of Hong Kong that creates a lot of issues I think for the Hong Hong Kong financial services community. So I come away from this these announcements feeling that this isn't great for Hong Kong's uh pledge to be, you know, a fully international crossber uh hub. Uh and and I worry a little bit about what this means um for for Hong Kong and its financial crossber um position, but also from an R&D standpoint, right? Because a lot of I think the optimism about Hong Kong from an R&D standpoint is look, we're going to get access to both the Chinese and the American leading models and we're going to be able to uh incorporate the the best of each each system. Well, that future is no longer uh apparent for Hong Kong and that I think quite remarkably changes how I think about Hong Kong uh moving forward. And to your point about ASML, I was looking at uh uh some images and videos of the huge equipment that is required and hyper complex uh both to to move and to maintain and they require ASML to be part of that process. It strikes me as very difficult for ASML and the Dutch to reject the American curbs on on semiconductor equipment restrictions per the match act. So I again I I I sort of am puzzled as to what Washington is trying to achieve. Are they are they politically motivated here or is there a real substance to these accusations? Because if it's politically motivated, are they trying to send a message to the Europeans to to other third party countries to say, "Hey, don't mess with our restrictions. This is a serious competition with China. We're watching you." >> On the ASML front, I really don't know. Um, you know, there seems to be a very strong disagreement between ASML and the US Commerce Department. This is sort of black and white. You either are exporting highly sensitive EUV technology to China or you're not. Um, of course, ASML has a huge presence in the China market and the China market is very important to ASML. But ASML is very well aware that it is banned from exporting EUV technology to China. There really isn't much ambiguity about that. Um, personally, I would be surprised uh if ASML has done this. Is it possible then that there's some kind of uh smuggling gone on? Again, very difficult because this is really really uh high-tech sensitive technology. you need a whole team of top engineers from ASML to travel with these semiconductor m uh tools. Uh you can't you can't sort of make it up um after it's arrived in China. So I'm puzzled by this. I'm genuinely puzzled. don't know what's happened, but I'm going to be watching it very carefully because as I said, if China has got hold of one of these EUV um tools, then uh I can't see any reason why it wouldn't be able to make the world's most advanced semiconductors. Obviously, after a period of time, >> if you were to assess based on these two developments, is China better off in this AI competition or not? I think in the short term this isn't great for a place like Hong Kong in the mainland. I think it's a bit of a wash. You know, it doesn't at all impede on the amount of uh development and capex that is going although arguably a lot less compared to the US 10 times less but still quite significant amount of capex that is going into AI development full speed ahead. And there's ways to keep distilling even if you ban it from the mainland and Hong Kong. But on the hardware side, I worry that this might be the first innings of even more restrictions that that we, you know, we saw the Pentagon ban more Chinese companies based on their military associations with the PLA. I I I worry that we're in the first innings of of further uh crackdown on on China um related export restrictions as it pertains to semiconductors and chips. uh now that David Saxs has left the White House, he was really I think one of the lone voices saying, "Hey, the export restrictions don't work." Um so I I worry that we may be in for more sustained tech uh competition. I think the first story about JP Morgan Chase not allowing uh its employees in Hong Kong to use the anthropic uh models is um is just an example of how peacemeal how kind of patchwork uh the US restrictions have been and how really largely futile they've also been. You can be a Chinese person in Singapore. You could be a Chinese person uh in the US or a Chinese company and you can easily get access to anthropic. If you want to train your Chinese LLM by using Claude or or one of the other anthropic models, you've got no restriction on you from doing that. You don't have to do it in Hong Kong. Um so I can't really see what uh Washington is aiming at. there doesn't seem to be joined up thinking on this. And I'd go one step further than that and say that when it comes to AI, uh the US has been ineffective, I think, in trying to hold China's progress back. I think we can say that maybe China is a few months behind the US on um making these LLMs, but I would say for sure that the US has been unable to um significantly hamper China's progress in making worldclass LLM so far. >> Okay, we'll be back with more after a quick break. Stay with us. >> Support for the show comes from LinkedIn. If you're a small business owner, you don't need me to tell you how much hiring great people matters. But the time and resources you have to spend to get it right are precious commodities. Sourcing, connecting with, and screening candidates can quickly eat into time better spent on your customers. That's where LinkedIn Hiring Pro comes in. It's designed to be your hiring partner, helping you source the right candidates faster. That way, you can hire with confidence without making it feel like a full-time job. LinkedIn Hiring Pro simplifies the entire process all the way from writing your job post to shortlisting candidates and running AI powered initial interviews. Plus, it does it all through a conversational interface where you can just describe what you're looking for in plain language. LinkedIn says nearly 60% of hirers find someone to interview within a week. With Hiring Pro, you spend less time searching and more time connecting with the right talent. So, instead of sifting through piles of RS, you get a tight, highquality short list that actually moves things forward. Join the 2.7 million small businesses using LinkedIn to hire. Get started by posting your job for free at linkedin.com/prov. Terms and conditions apply. Welcome back. Active web brand Lululemon recently held a promotional event for the brand at the Great Wall of China. It was buil as a celebration of Chinese culture, but the intent backfired badly when a Japanese style drum appeared in promotional imagery, triggering a nationalist backlash on Chinese social media that topped 50 million views on Waybo. James, this is not the first instantiation of some, I would say, uh, hackneed and ill-faded uh, PR done by a western company in China. Uh I was I looked at some of the pictures. They're very impressive. If anyone wants to see Lululemon mats on the Great Wall of China, people doing yoga. It's a very impressive image. But it seemed like somebody dropped the memo internally about including a Japanese drum at a point in time where I think Japan China relations are as bad as we've ever seen really since 2010 2011. you know, when you started to have the Saku Islands incident and the rare earth embargo, I think we're in a similar patch of really bad Japan China relations. So, the timing couldn't be worse. James, what was your reaction when you saw the imagery? >> You know, to be honest with you, I feel a bit sorry for Lululemon here because uh is this actually a Japanese style drum? It does seem to be that does seem to be a question. And I mean certainly uh this this is a big double-sided drum. Uh it was played by a top tier Chinese actor called uh Juilong. But what happened was people online in China began to say, "Oh, this drum uh looks like a Japanese wad." And then this uh clip went viral on social media. And so that's where the allegation of this being a Japanese drum uh really started. Then according to the Chinese company that had been responsible for getting this drum onto the Great Wall, they then said, "Oh, well hang on. This is not a Japanese drum. This drum is a replica of the Jer drum from the Tang Dynasty. That's a Chinese dynasty that ran from 6 618 until 9007. Um, and apparently the J people, apparently they're an ancient ethnic minority living in the northwestern part of China. And uh, the drum, this type of drum was supposed to have been praised by the Chinese emperor of the time. So to me, there is a real question as to whether or not this is an old Chinese drum. that was perhaps borrowed by the Japanese but its antecedants were first Chinese. Um but then the nationalists in China online have been you know uh getting very agitated about it and that's why Lululemon had to apologize as they now have done. They've apologized for this marketing campaign and for using the drum. So I suppose the moral of this whole story is if you are a brand operating in China, be very careful because maybe the truth of these matters is less important than what the online nationalists could construe as the truth. And once you get into that situation, I've seen it so many times in China, once you have the online uh activist building up ahead of steam, it doesn't matter what the truth is, you just have to apologize and pull the marketing campaign. And that's what's happened in this case. >> Yeah, I I read a similar backstory to this and I'm glad you raised it, but Lululemon has come out and and obviously apologized. You know, China is a huge and and growing market for Lululemon. Actually, I've been really surprised by how successful they've been in China. Um, who would have thought that this, you know, very um West Coast athleisure brand would find inroads, but it reflects a growing fitness wellness trend in China in the middle to upper middle and upper classes uh that Lulle Lemon's really trying to tap into. It actually reminded me to some extent of some previous campaigns which we can get into which I thought were actually worse or at least they were not as well they were not as in good taste is what I mean they were in more they were in poor taste frankly and one of them I distinctly remember back in 2018 the Dolan Gabbana ads I don't know if you saw the Chinese model eating a pizza with Chinese chopsticks nizens in China got really offended uh by that I won't weigh in but certainly people were very heated uh and that led led to huge boycotts and I think D&G Doan Gabbana hasn't really revived itself since that 2018 fiasco and then we had a Versace coach Jivanchi issue in 2019 where we had t-shirts that listed Hong Kong and Macau as countries separate from China. Again, this created huge verality and complaints on way and Chinese social media and you know several Chinese uh brand ambassadors canceled their contracts publicly with the brands. Uh these are just a couple but it reflects the fact that when you're a brand even if you're domestic you have you have to deal with a very heated uh population of consumers and nizens with strong opinions and your point is completely spot on James if they believe something to be especially if they believe it to be anti-China uh or against their cultural country they will come out in in in spades and complain and Because China is a huge market, you know, 1.4 trillion people, but but a huge consumption uh engine, a lot of these brands have to count out ultimately because they want to protect their bottom line. >> You know, the regularity of these flare-ups is really quite remarkable. Even in April this year, the French uh fashion brand Laame Mer apologized after its advertisement caused a public backlash because of a an image of of a long braid and and a white long gown. This is supposed to or you know was seen as alluding to a period of uh humiliation in Chinese history in the 19th century and I suppose uh at that time uh people would have worn uh costume like that. There was also in 2021 there was an H&M, Nike, Burberry and Adidas issue. In 2022 there was a Dior issue. In 2025 there was an Arcturix uh issue. So this is something that the foreign brands seem to get into on a very regular basis and I just wonder whether there's a mismatch of communication between the China office and the head office. These are big mistakes. I mean, you know, China is a huge market for these luxury brands, and you can poison your reputation in China with the merest of slip ups, uh, cultural slip ups or political slip ups if you claim that, you know, Taiwan is, uh, is is a separate country or something like that. So um I guess this is another cautionary tale and another reminder to the big western companies that they should really look at their internal controls when it comes to China and marketing. >> Okay, let's take one last quick break and stay with us. >> Support for the show comes from SoFi. If you're a parent helping your child navigate college, you know just how fast the costs can pile up. Between tuition, housing, textbooks, and everyday living expenses, funding higher education is a major financial commitment. That's where today's sponsor, SoFi, comes in. SoFi offers private student loans that can cover up to 100% of school certified costs. Not just undergrad and graduate tuition, but SoFi can also help cover housing, books, food, and other education related expenses. SoFi's private student loans are all about flexibility. They offer competitive fixed or variable rates, multiple term options, and monthly payments so you can build around your budget. The application process is completely online, and you can check your rate in minutes. And for families exploring their options together, adding a qualified co-signer may help improve chances of securing a lower interest rate. And there are zero fees required. That means no origination fees or late fees, no surprises. What you see is what you pay back. Head to sofi.com/proofg student to check your options and get your education funded the smarter way. That's sofi.com/propg student. Originated by SoFi Bank NA member FDIC. Terms and conditions apply. Please borrow responsibly. Well, welcome back. Finally, we have to talk about the World Cup today. China failed to qualify this year. In fact, it has only been in the World Cup once in 2002. But football fans in China have found something else to cheer about. The World Cup referee Maning Manning is huge in China right now because he's the only Chinese guy being represented in the FIFA World Cup and he's a referee. Uh he apparently has now hundreds of thousands of followers on social media platforms. He's kind of seen been belovedly called the Cardman. uh because he's quite strict in his delivery of the rules as a referee. It's interesting because you know clearly there is a big social media and online following and presence that's happening for the World Cup. Uh but it's it's shifted from I would say previous years, right? I think so much of this is now online short form. We we previously speculated that China might not even have the rights to disseminate the World Cup in mainland China, but it seems that in the last innings there was finally a deal um worked out by China Media Group, a stateowned media organization that inked the broadcast rights uh with FIFA for the World Cup. And Shahong, which is China's Instagram, is effectively the strategic partner to China Media Group. they won the distribution rights to co-stream the World Cup games for free to all users. So a lot of effectively you know millions of Chinese uh you know watching this uh online on their phones some on the TV. The big question is you know why isn't China I think better at soccer. I I have some theories but I wanted to get your take quick take on that and whether or not you know you think this is a growing industry because after all Xi Jinping is a huge stand of football. We know this given his his his pledge to to make China a real football power. And yet we have, you know, haven't really seen great football uh participation from the Chinese since 2002. So what explains all that, James? >> I wish I knew. Um Cinping in 2011, uh he's famous for saying that he had three wishes. He wanted China to qualify for the World Cup. He wants China to host a World Cup. and he wants China to win a World Cup. Um, and I'm afraid China has uh is really falling down badly on all of those wishes at the moment. I remember when I was a university student in China, uh, people used to play football every day, all hours of the day. Um, our uh, dormitories looked onto the football pitch and it was I mean, of course, they had stop at dark, but it was constant. And I've never lived in a country that is as football mad as China. And I come from the UK. We're pretty football mad over here. But China is more football mad, I would like to say, than the UK. People are fascinated by it. It's very regular that you'll meet somebody or you'll be talking to a taxi driver who will not only know all of the top teams in the UK, they'll know all the top teams in Spain, in Italy, uh in America, they'll have they'll know some of the top players. They're very very well informed on China. So for China not to have qualified for the World Cup uh since 2002 obviously it's a big disappointment for Chinese people and the other juosition I think is relevant is that China's very good at sport. China won the Olympic Games in terms of the medal count in 2008. Um, and I believe every year since then, it's played second fiddle to the United States, which as we all know is excellent in athletics and and other sports like that. So, what explains the fact that China has been so poor when it comes to the World Cup? Well, a lot of different people have different views. there is definitely corruption running through Chinese football, Chinese soccer and uh so perhaps that is part of it. Uh perhaps there's a problem with the coaching um perhaps there's a problem with the pathways but given that it is the case as you've said Alice uh the focus of 1.4 four billion people in China watching this World Cup unfold has turned to Maning the the referee. He's called the card master in China because he doesn't take any disobedience on the pitch. He just doss out the cards. In one game, he doled out nine cards. And the Chinese um you know, football fans, soccer fans, they love him for that. So maybe maybe Manning uh he goes somewhere in this World Cup. Maybe he he gets close to refing a final. I I I don't know. But um it's an interesting situation and um even though China's not at the World Cup, it is certainly galvanized by the spectacle unfolding in the US and the other countries that are hosting. >> Yeah, it's fascinating your point. The the football enthusiasm is there in the everyday people. You know, this is why I remember a couple years ago, uh, Shingping had this national initiative to get all kids to play soccer. Uh, and I remember seeing videos of him playing soccer. This is a big thing precoid to to try to get everyday people, especially children healthy playing soccer, making sure that China would have um, great teams to go into the World Cup. It still hasn't since since she launched that initiative. I have this pet theory that I I I'm borrowing from uh a friend of mine who's an economist in China, David Lee, who has this theory that it's all about economics at the end of the day. So, everyday Chinese parents would rather the ROI of investing in an athlete who is doing an individual sport like golf or tennis or skiing like Eingu or gymnastics because then they can control the variables. they can they can know from a very early age if they're genetically, you know, physically gifted for that sport. Uh, and then they can um cash in on the prize money, whereas it's less sure if you're going to do it for a team sport, if you're even going to be able to qualify for a team and then what the future payouts will be. It's hyperdependent on the team performance, not just the individual performance. So I I'm borrowing that idea because I think that that my suspicion is that economics play a role in China being generally very good and you see this in the Olympics at individual sports and not so much at team sports like soccer, basketball. That explains to me why China's been so bad because really they haven't and so far as I understand James correct me they haven't won a single goal. >> That's right. uh not since 1938 has China scored a goal at the uh World Cup and it's the only qualifying country with that dubious distinction. So it is really uh a bit of a disaster. One other thing that I'll mention is that it's not just that Chinese the Instagram was streaming the performance for millions of of users in China. The other thing that was worth mentioning is that the CCTV's app for streaming the World Cup was the second most downloaded app this past week on China's Apple App Store. And the official sports betting app was ranked sixth most downloaded. Uh and then Chinese tech companies like Tencent Cloud are actually responsible for 2/3 of the official World Cup broadcasting in APAC. behind the scenes at the back end. We've got Chinese tech companies that are involved in the distribution and streaming of of the FIFA World Cup. All right, James, you know what time it is. It's prediction time. As you peer into the future this week, what do you see? >> Okay, Alice, I am sticking with foot with football, with soccer. Um, and um I'm going to stick my neck out and say China will qualify for the next World Cup. And the reason I'm forwarding for this is that there is actually an interesting uh new model of uh football of soccer in in China. Um and this is the Djang Soo Football City League. It's known as the Sue Super League. And the difference between this league and the way that the the game is played in the rest of China is that this is bottom up. And uh this is in stark contrast to the topdown government attempts to build a football superpower that you have in the rest of the company. So in Djang Su province you have this league. It draws uh teams from local residents and students and it's creating you know a real buzz. In my view this is the only way uh to run a football uh system. You have to have kids all over the country galvanized um and playing um you know playing in a carefree way uh playing in their local clubs uh and not being kind of handpicked by uh government mandarins from an early age and put intomies and and all of that. I mean, think about how Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo, even Pelle back in the day for Brazil, how they grew up. They grew up playing football in the streets. Diego Maradona was the same, right? So, this Djang Su Football City League, I think, I'm putting my hopes on it anyway, um, is creating a whole new football culture in China. And that's why I'm going to stick my neck out and say next World Cup China qualifies. >> Okay, it's a bold claim. We'll have to we'll have to check that out. So mine is in the realm of Iran and what's happening in the Middle East. We haven't discussed it yet, but we've gotten a memorandum of understanding between the Americans and the Iranians. Uh what does that mean for the future of uh the region, especially if you zoom out for the Gulf States? That remains to be seen. But I I think that one of the big geopolitical winners from this whole Iran fiasco is China long term. Uh the Gulf States will hedge even more and get closer to the Chinese. We're already starting to see that in some public announcements and trips. That concretely means I think that some of the Chinese telecommunications tech AI companies even the AI model companies in China will have an inroads in the Gulf States and building out their new smart cities. And that is key for for these Chinese tech companies that are being you know ring fenced in other parts of the world notably America. So I think we'll start to see greater integration of technology stacks and and systems between the Gulf States and the Chinese. Uh and I feel that coming out of this conflict will see a more geopolitically influential China in in the region. All right, that's all for this episode. Thank you for listening to China Decode. This is a production of Prof Media. Make sure to follow us wherever you get your podcast so you don't miss an episode. We'll talk to you again next week.
